speaker: interviewer
text: And also, you're going to tell me everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: Date is November the 23rd, 1995. The survivor is Syda Selinger. Interviewer is Barbara Linz. And we're in Sydney, Australia. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November the 23rd, 1995. My name is Barbara Linz, L-I-N-Z. And I'm interviewing Syda Selinger in Sydney, Australia. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name and how you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Syda, S-Y-D-A, Selinger, S-E-L-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Florenz, F-L-O-R-E-N-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's just that instead of Syda they used to call me Sydja.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when? What, what is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22Nd of April, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we start by talking about your childhood? Tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very happy childhood. I can go back only as far as I can remember, and that would be about when I was four or five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob, in English-- Jakob in Polish, [? Jakov ?] in Hebrew. My mother's name was Bluma Hinde. She was born Leineer, L-E-I-N-E-E-R. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had a brother, Beno, in Hebrew, [? Benzion ?] Florenz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1914, on the 13th of April. And I had a sister, Eda, E-D-A. And she was born in the 12th of September, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most wonderful person I have ever met as a person, and a very, very caring father. I have never, with all honesty, never have I heard my father raising his voice, yelling at anybody. He achieved, always, in bringing up his children by communicating with us, even though he was a very, very orthodox, religious man. He knew how to get through to us and convey whatever he wanted to to us in such a way that I could never, ever lie to him, nor to my mother, for that matter. But that's how we were brought up. I have never heard any arguments at home. It was just the most pleasant, the most happy atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did my father do? Well, first of all, he was a partner in a bank. He was-- he had properties. And he also had a like an agency for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Vienna, there was a, a very known factory of [INAUDIBLE] hats. And he was related sort of in that business with somebody else, too. So we were well off. But we didn't know, like, we children did know, what our parents had or didn't have. To them, we were brought up to be like all other children around us. Which, in a way, was good. Because when you go to school, you're mixed with rich children and poor children. In the building-- which belonged to my parents, where I was born and I grew up-- there were poor and rich. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So because that was my parents' thinking, because we didn't know in which way we are different to those children. We were just one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jews in that building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the building where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were Christians, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the relations like between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with the Christians in the building and with the girls at school, we had a very good to relationship, so much so that when my father used to go to shul, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they were throwing stones after Jews, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the boys from the non-Jewish families in our building were standing there, don't throw a stone at Mr. Florenz. There was a reason behind it. They were poor, very poor. They couldn't pay the rent. And my father would never sort of put them out into the street. He said if they could, they would pay. He is-- it was in, in the family, like my grandfather, too. They were a very philanthropic family. They belonged to the known philanthropic societies. We had, one a year, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- not-- when I say we, I mean my parents and my grandfathers, my grandparents-- invite, I can't be precise, whether it was 200 people or 300 people. And we had cooks coming and cooking. And the rabbi of Krakow would come. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi Kolnice. And that was like a banquet. But people gave donations for the poor Jews in Krakow. There were lots of them. You won't believe me, but I only learned from my brother after the war that the-- this property or the other property belonged to us. I didn't know. He was eight years older, so of course, he was already brought into the picture by my parents. But we were too young to know it. I remember distinctly-- for-- I'll give you a small example. Oranges didn't grow in Poland, or bananas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my parents-- they were imported. And my parents were buying them. And so I would have an orange. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would say, can I have the-- an orange to school for lunch? And my mother-- and only now when you grow older, you understand what they were trying to tell you-- my mother said, no, you can't. I said, why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because other children may not have orange. They probably haven't seen an orange. And they will see you eating an orange. And you can't divide one orange into 30. So you will have it at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I love her for it now, but at that time, it seemed a bit strange to me. Why can't I have it, if I have it, you know? So it's-- but everything was said and done in a quiet, loving way. And that is, perhaps, why I tried to do the same when we were bringing up Jake, our son-- needless to say, our everything in the world. I-- You said your father was religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was he Hasidic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hasidic? He didn't follow any rabbi. He, he didn't believe in the Kabbalistic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reasoning and studying. But he, he was forever studying the Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, I asked my father once-- because he slept till 12 o'clock, midnight, and so did my grandfather. And then he used to get up and go to his study and pulled out from that huge library, his-- the Mishnah and this and that-- the parts of the Torah. And once, like a silly girl, I asked, Father, how is it that you forever, as far as I can remember, you always study in the afternoon and then at night? You must know it by heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father said, you know, the more you learn, the more you found-- find out how little you know. And yes, that's true, too. And he was showing me how it, it deals with, with not only religion as such, it deals with every subject-- with hygiene, and medicine, and, and mathematics. And it was there. And he, he knew how to combine the religion with knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was his library mainly Jewish books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only-- German and Polish. Polish was not so rich, but mainly the Hebrew and German, because my parents were born and raised under the Austro-Hungarian [? emporium. ?] So they attended German schools, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents spoke either German, or Yiddish, or Polish. Between themselves, they didn't speak Polish. But I spoke to my father in Polish and to my mother in Polish. If I ever had a problem with an expression when I was doing an essay, it was my father, the highest authority. He wouldn't rest until he found in the lexicon or the dictionary the right answer for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to, to me, he was absolutely the highest authority. And I loved him for being what he was. He was religious. We didn't have, at home, pictures of faces, only landscapes. He wouldn't shake hands with women-- hand with women. He, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he, he was-- how-- it's very hard to explain. Because he was a very intelligent person, and yet a very religious person, which was very rare. Because religious people see only their own circle, whereas my father was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very knowledgeable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And having to deal with business, he had these solicitors. And if a solicitor did something wrong, my father would say, no, the law says this and that and that. He would have law books and this sort of thing. And he was a remarkable man. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother equally as religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but she, she was a very pedantic woman. She was, she was-- she put on us a certain rigor, particularly on the girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You had to walk properly. And you had to speak properly. And you had to sit properly. And you had to-- you know, sometimes it was annoying. But Mother, thank you for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The schools you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you. What school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- the primary school was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't a private school a government school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- mixed, Christians and Jewish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have non-Jewish friends at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My father didn't mind. He's-- my father had a principle that if you want to live in peace with other people from other denominations, you had to respect their beliefs. And they will respect your beliefs. And at school, before Christmas, the Christian girls had Christmas lunch before Christmas Eve, right, the day before. And the priests has come. And they had [? lungfish, ?] herring, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was invited as a representative of the Jewish girls. That was, to me, a problem. And I came home-- but the good thing about the upbringing was that I could talk to my parents. And I sat down with my mother and father. And I told them, that's the situation. What do I do? I don't want to ignore them. And my father said, no, you won't. You will go. I opened my eyes really wide. And I thought, well, what's next?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father said, you will take your plate, your cutlery, your fish, and you will sit down and eat with them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I sort of was a little bit-- I felt a bit strange about it, coming with my own food. But my father said, don't feel badly about it, nothing to be ashamed of. Just come and say what I told you, which I did. Luckily, the school was just across the road. And you know, when the priest said, how nice of you. It's really nice to join us to celebrate with us with your own food according to your religion. So that's how I was brought up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever anti-Semitism at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I haven't noticed any. We were sort of an equal number. I had girls, two or three non-Jewish girls, coming always to copy my homework. But they didn't understand. Could they copy it? And they lived next door, next building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, we were very friendly. It was-- they knew Yom Kippur or Rash Hashanah, they are not to come because we are not eating. We are fasting. And we go to shul. And-- it-- no, I think-- but anti-Semitism was not strange to me. Because my cousin, for example, who lived in the same building, she was already studying law at university, at Krakow University. And there were always, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how shall I say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] riots. They had to call an ambulance. The Jewish students were injured. And, and they used to call out, out with the Jews, in with Jewesses. They liked the Jewish girls, because they were attractive and clever. That doesn't go to say that the non-Jewish were not clever. But that was the general idea, that the Jews are clever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in high school? Where-- did you go to a Jewish high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, I? Yes, I went to a Hebrew high school. And that was a, a, a dilemma. Because I did want to study, at first, medicine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Speaking of medicine, I had an uncle who was a very Orthodox Jew and was a professor in medicine. See, you can combine things. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was so interested in it that I wanted to study medicine. However, there were two reasons for which I didn't. First of all, in the year that I was eligible to go to university, Jews were not admitted anymore to the faculty. It was a numerus nullus for Jews. Secondly, I was very interested in languages. That was a separate school. But my father didn't subscribe to any of it, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were reasons. He had his own reasons. I would mix with the non-Jewish boys. I may fell in love with a Jew-- non-Jewish boy. I would have to go on Saturday to the library, or wherever. And that was not [INAUDIBLE]. I wasn't quite happy about it. But in those days, we didn't rebel. That's what parents said. And that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was another-- it was a-- like a school that taught you the same. It was a Hebrew school. And it was approved by the Department of Education. But the bad side of it was that it was a Zionist school. So, my father wasn't a Zionist. Of course, [? Messiah ?] will come and nobody else will take us there. So it was like to choose between the devil and the deep sea. So all the Jews, my, my girlfriends' fathers, said, well, what the Florenzes will do, we will do, too. So my father said, out of the two evils, I'll much rather have a Zionist school. Because at least she's among Jewish people. And we can deal with the-- if she becomes, or, or is on the way to become a Zionist-- we will talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that's how I finished up in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I graduated in 1939. I never thought we had to-- we had 27 subjects. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to have three foreign languages. Hebrew was one of them. That was compulsory. The others, you could choose. So German was easy for me. And the third, I thought, well, I'll choose English. Because somehow, it seems like it belongs to the Anglo-Saxon groups of languages. So it would be probably something related to the German language. And so I did. But to be honest with you, I never thought I'll ever, ever use the language. I never thought I'll ever leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, did your parents know what was happening in Germany with the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes-- we were reading about it in the papers. But to really have the first news was when, once, in the middle of the night, the doorbell rang. My father-- I have to go back to put you into the picture-- my father had a cousin in Frankfurt. And he was married to a Rothschild. And I didn't quite understand why they were not very close, my father and this cousin. Never asked, I-- just a New Year's card, no correspondence. But one night-- I think it was in 1937 or '38, '38 maybe-- in the middle of the night, the doorbell rang. And the maid opened the door, woke up my mother, and says, there's a couple, a man and a woman, at the door. And they said that their name is Grossinger. And they are cousins from Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my father got up, went to see who it is. They were crying. They had overnight bags with them. They were simply kicked out of Germany, just within five minutes. And they had to cross the border. And they left them in [INAUDIBLE] on the border. And they didn't know where to go or to whom to turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they remembered us-- my parents, not me. And they came. And they told us what, what is really happening in Germany. Which year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which year? Well, as I said, I'm not quite sure whether it was '37 or '38. I really not quite sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, quietly, I asked my mother, how come we never correspond or anything with them? She wouldn't quite tell me. But now I know, the Rothschild were a converted since generations. And he obviously came into the term of marrying out sort of thing. And it wasn't to this cousin's credit or, you know? It was just-- so they-- So how did your parents react to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they gave them shelter. Yes, they made up beds. And they stayed with us for quite awhile until they were able to sell-- I don't know, they had with them valuables, or something. And then they rented a flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break there for the end of the tape.
